system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task G-10: ICU triage protocol under sustained capacity overflow (extended)

## Context

Same as Task G: regional health system, 240 ICU beds, 6 weeks of overflow, exhausted staff, need formal triage protocol within 7 days. Ten candidate protocols instead of the original 5. **Choose one as the adopted protocol.**

## Options

**Option A: Predicted short-term survival** - SOFA score + 28-day survival prediction as sole ranking.

**Option B: Predicted long-term outcome** - SOFA + QALY-weighted post-recovery prediction.

**Option C: First-come-first-served with medical floor** - procedural fairness, minimal discretion, no withdrawal except medical futility.

**Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties** - survival 40%, life-cycle 20%, instrumental value 20%, pandemic factors 20%, lottery on ties.

**Option E: Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest** - small list of medical exclusions, then daily lottery among eligible.

**Option F: Patient-and-family co-decision** - admission requires multidisciplinary team + patient/proxy decision. Withdrawal requires consensus including family. Outcomes: dignified; aligns with autonomy ethics; reduces moral injury via shared responsibility. Risks: slow; emotional burden on families; outcomes depend on family resources; resource-intensive for clinical team.

**Option G: Trial-of-therapy with mandated time limits** - everyone admitted for trial period (e.g., 5-7 days). Withdrawal only after pre-defined time without improvement, evaluated against pre-defined criteria. Outcomes: avoids initial exclusion; transparent timeline; ethically defensible. Risks: still requires withdrawal decisions; can prolong suffering; uses bed days that might save more lives elsewhere.

**Option H: Tiered system based on resource scarcity** - dynamic protocol: at <80% capacity use Option C, 80-95% switch to Option A, >95% switch to Option D. Re-evaluate weekly. Outcomes: adaptive to actual scarcity; matches ethical framework to severity; precedent in disaster medicine. Risks: complex to implement; staff training burden; tier transitions are politically explosive moments.

**Option I: Community-deliberated framework** - convene a citizen jury (25-40 community members, diverse) over a weekend to deliberate and propose criteria. Adopt their recommendation. Outcomes: democratic legitimacy; reflects community values; precedent in Oregon health care priorities. Risks: takes >7 days; jury may produce internally inconsistent framework; may produce framework medical leadership cannot endorse.

**Option J: Pre-pandemic preference registry** - patients pre-register their preferences for ICU care under scarcity (full intervention vs comfort-focused). During overflow, registered preferences guide allocation. Default for unregistered = full intervention. Outcomes: respects individual autonomy ex ante; transparent; preserves trust. Risks: registration uptake will be biased (well-resourced patients register more); changes nature of medical encounter into bureaucratic; doesn't apply to acute presentations.

## What to provide

Choose one as the adopted protocol. Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific patient case would make you regret this choice.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
Given the sustained overload, exhausted staff, and 7‑day deadline, **Option E – Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest** – is the most defensible and implementable protocol.

A triage system under these conditions must be simple, procedurally transparent, and minimally corrosive to clinician morale. Option E achieves this by requiring only two steps: first, apply a small, pre‑defined list of medical exclusion criteria (e.g., imminent death regardless of ICU, or conditions so mild that medical‑floor care suffices); second, allocate remaining beds by a daily lottery among all eligible patients. This eliminates the bedside moral calculus of who “deserves” to survive, shifting the burden of discrimination onto a visible, fair‑chance mechanism. For exhausted staff, a lottery offers psychological protection: they are not forced to rank the value of lives, merely to disclose that every eligible person has an equal opportunity. Implementation requires no complex scoring, no nuanced prognostic modelling, and minimal training – a working group can finalise the exclusion list within a few days. Public trust, though tested, is better preserved when the system admits its limits and resorts to impartial luck rather than subjective weighing.

The most compelling alternative, Option A (predicted short‑term survival via SOFA and 28‑day prediction), promises to maximise lives saved – a powerful utilitarian appeal. Yet it demands that worn‑down clinicians repeatedly adjudicate fine prognostic differences, often on incomplete data, while knowing that small score variances will deny care to someone who might live. This fuels moral injury and risks systematic disadvantage to those with chronic illness or disabilities, whose baseline scores are already elevated. Moreover, survival predictions in a pandemic are notoriously unstable; a lottery avoids anchoring allocation to a flawed statistical phantom. While Option A might produce a higher raw survival count, it does so at the cost of embedding value judgments that exhausted teams are ill‑equipped to sustain. Option E sacrifices some efficiency for equity and psychological sustainability – a trade‑off that acknowledges the humanity of both patients and caregivers.

The patient case that would make me regret this choice is a previously healthy young adult with a treatable acute deterioration who loses the lottery to an older, multi‑morbid patient with a <15% chance of surviving to discharge. The younger patient’s death, directly attributable to chance, will feel profoundly unjust. But that sting is the price of a system that refuses to categorically devalue any life. In a crisis where any allocation method will produce heartbreak, a transparent lottery at least ensures that no group is written off a priori, and that the regret is shared by the society that designed it – not borne solely by the clinicians who enforced it.

message.reasoning:
We need to choose one protocol from 10 options (A-J) for ICU triage under sustained capacity overflow, with context of 240 ICU beds, 6 weeks overflow, exhausted staff, 7 days to implement. We must advocate for one, provide reasoning why, address why not the most compelling alternative, and a specific patient case that would make us regret the choice.

I need to evaluate each option briefly, but the answer should be a single recommendation with reasoning, not a list. The prompt: "Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options." So I'll pick one, explain why it's the best, mention a compelling alternative and why I didn't choose it, and a regret case.

First, consider the context: sustained overflow, 6 weeks already, staff exhausted. Need a formal triage protocol within 7 days. So time pressure, but 7 days is enough for rapid implementation, not a long process. The system is already under stress, so simplicity, fairness, and minimizing moral injury are key. The protocol must be ethically defensible and practically implementable with exhausted staff.

Looking at options:

A: Predicted short-term survival – SOFA + 28-day survival prediction. Efficient, evidence-based, maximizes lives saved in the short term? Might be good for immediate crisis, but could disadvantage those with chronic conditions or disability, raising equity concerns. But it's straightforward.

B: Predicted long-term outcome with QALY. Introduces quality-of-life weighting, which is controversial and could discriminate against elderly, disabled. Not ideal for quick consensus, and staff might find it morally distressing.

C: First-come-first-served with medical floor – procedural fairness, no withdrawal except futility. Simple, no discriminatory judgments, but ignores survival benefit. In overflow, might allocate beds to those with low chance of survival just because they arrived first, potentially causing more deaths overall. Might feel fair but lead to worse outcomes.

D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery – complex scoring, lottery on ties. Balances survival, life-cycle, instrumental value, pandemic factors. This is thorough but very complex to implement with exhausted staff, might cause disagreements about weighting, and requires training. Could be too cumbersome.

E: Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest – small list of exclusions, then lottery. Avoids judgments about individual worth beyond basic medical exclusions. Simple, transparent, random allocation among those with some potential to benefit. But it might still exclude some who could benefit marginally, and lottery could be seen as arbitrary.

F: Patient-and-family co-decision – requires team and patient/proxy decision for admission and withdrawal. Dignified, respects autonomy, reduces moral injury through shared responsibility. But risky: slow, emotionally burdensome, resource-intensive, outcomes depend on family resources. In exhausted state, this seems impossible to implement within 7 days, and would add immense burden to clinicians and families. Not suitable for sustained overflow where quick decisions are needed.

G: Trial-of-therapy with mandated time limits – everyone gets a trial period, then withdrawal after no improvement with predefined criteria. Avoids initial exclusion, transparent timeline. But still requires withdrawal decisions, can prolong suffering, uses bed days that might save others. Might cause conflict if families object to withdrawal. Could be ethically defensible but emotionally taxing. In exhausted staff, might be hard to enforce time limits consistently, and could lead to painful disputes.

H: Tiered system based on resource scarcity – dynamic, from C to A to D as capacity worsens. Adaptive, but complex to implement, requires monitoring capacity tiers, training, and politically explosive transitions. With exhausted staff, a dynamic tiered system might add operational confusion, and transitions could cause chaos. Not ideal when everyone is already burnt out; they need a stable protocol.

I: Community-deliberated framework – citizen jury. Democratic, reflects community values, but takes >7 days (they say it takes >7 days, so not feasible within the timeline). Also may produce inconsistent framework, and medical leadership may not endorse it. So time constraint rules it out.

J: Pre-pandemic preference registry – patients pre-register preferences. Not feasible for acute presentations without prior registration, uptake biased, and doesn't apply to those who haven't registered. In an ongoing crisis with new admissions likely many unregistered, it's impractical. Also doesn't solve the triage problem for those without registries, and changes encounter into bureaucratic.

Thus, most feasible and ethically defensible given constraints is likely Option E: Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest. Why? Simple, transparent, no individual judgments beyond medical exclusions (like patients too sick to benefit, or too well to need ICU). Lottery gives equal chance to all eligible, avoids discrimination, and is procedurally fair. It reduces moral injury because clinicians aren't making complex life-and-death judgments based on subjective criteria; they just apply clear medical exclusion criteria and then randomization. It can be implemented quickly. Exhausted staff can follow a simple algorithm: check exclusion list (e.g., severe irreversible organ failure, terminal illness with <6m survival?), if no, enter lottery. Daily lottery for available beds. This avoids the "which patient is more valuable" dilemma that leads to moral distress. It's transparent to public, defensible.

But I need to address the most compelling alternative. Likely Option A (predicted survival) or Option D (multi-principle) or Option C. For me, Option A uses SOFA and 28-day survival prediction, aiming to save most lives. It's compelling because it maximizes utility, but it could discriminate against those with higher SOFA scores due to chronic conditions, and predictions are imperfect. It requires clinicians to rank patients by a score that might not capture all factors, and could lead to denial of ICU to someone with a low chance but who might still survive. In a lottery, everyone with some chance gets a chance, which respects equality. So I'll argue that E is better than A because it avoids utilitarian discrimination and is simpler to apply fairly without complex scoring. But I need to pick one alternative to compare. The prompt: "why not the most compelling alternative." I can choose the most compelling alternative to E, which I think is either A or C. C (first-come-first-served) is also procedurally fair, but it benefits those who happen to arrive earlier, which might be arbitrary relative to medical need. E at least screens for medical exclusions, so only those who could potentially benefit are in the lottery, not all comers. So E is better than C.

I'll advocate for Option E.

Now, the specific patient case that would make me regret this choice: a young, otherwise healthy patient with a high chance of survival if given ICU care, but who loses the lottery to an older patient with multiple comorbidities who has a low chance of survival. That would feel tragic, because the lottery might allocate a bed to someone who is likely to die anyway, while a saveable patient dies. That would make me regret not choosing a method that prioritizes survival probability. But I'll argue that the regret is mitigated by the fairness of the lottery and the need to avoid systematic discrimination. I'll frame it as a trade-off.

The answer should be 200-400 words, advocating for Option E. I'll craft it.

messages.content: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.

# Task G-10: ICU triage protocol under sustained capacity overflow (extended)

## Context

Same as Task G: regional health system, 240 ICU beds, 6 weeks of overflow, exhausted staff, need formal triage protocol within 7 days. Ten candidate protocols instead of the original 5. **Choose one as the adopted protocol.**

## Options

**Option A: Predicted short-term survival** - SOFA score + 28-day survival prediction as sole ranking.

**Option B: Predicted long-term outcome** - SOFA + QALY-weighted post-recovery prediction.

**Option C: First-come-first-served with medical floor** - procedural fairness, minimal discretion, no withdrawal except medical futility.

**Option D: Multi-principle scoring with lottery on ties** - survival 40%, life-cycle 20%, instrumental value 20%, pandemic factors 20%, lottery on ties.

**Option E: Categorical exclusion + lottery for the rest** - small list of medical exclusions, then daily lottery among eligible.

**Option F: Patient-and-family co-decision** - admission requires multidisciplinary team + patient/proxy decision. Withdrawal requires consensus including family. Outcomes: dignified; aligns with autonomy ethics; reduces moral injury via shared responsibility. Risks: slow; emotional burden on families; outcomes depend on family resources; resource-intensive for clinical team.

**Option G: Trial-of-therapy with mandated time limits** - everyone admitted for trial period (e.g., 5-7 days). Withdrawal only after pre-defined time without improvement, evaluated against pre-defined criteria. Outcomes: avoids initial exclusion; transparent timeline; ethically defensible. Risks: still requires withdrawal decisions; can prolong suffering; uses bed days that might save more lives elsewhere.

**Option H: Tiered system based on resource scarcity** - dynamic protocol: at <80% capacity use Option C, 80-95% switch to Option A, >95% switch to Option D. Re-evaluate weekly. Outcomes: adaptive to actual scarcity; matches ethical framework to severity; precedent in disaster medicine. Risks: complex to implement; staff training burden; tier transitions are politically explosive moments.

**Option I: Community-deliberated framework** - convene a citizen jury (25-40 community members, diverse) over a weekend to deliberate and propose criteria. Adopt their recommendation. Outcomes: democratic legitimacy; reflects community values; precedent in Oregon health care priorities. Risks: takes >7 days; jury may produce internally inconsistent framework; may produce framework medical leadership cannot endorse.

**Option J: Pre-pandemic preference registry** - patients pre-register their preferences for ICU care under scarcity (full intervention vs comfort-focused). During overflow, registered preferences guide allocation. Default for unregistered = full intervention. Outcomes: respects individual autonomy ex ante; transparent; preserves trust. Risks: registration uptake will be biased (well-resourced patients register more); changes nature of medical encounter into bureaucratic; doesn't apply to acute presentations.

## What to provide

Choose one as the adopted protocol. Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this protocol, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific patient case would make you regret this choice.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
